landing.heroTitle: Virtual labs with lessons, not just sliders
landing.heroSubtitle: LabSim combines interactive 3D experiments, structured lesson content, and an AI tutor—so students connect theory, prediction, and observation in one place.
landing.bullet3: Live data tied to what you see
landing.howLead: A simple loop: read, manipulate, observe, explain—supported by the sim and the tutor.
landing.step1Text: Each lab pairs objectives, readings, and checkpoints with a live 3D simulation.
landing.step2Text: Change variables, predict outcomes, then press Play to see motion and measured data together.
landing.step3Text: Ask Socratic questions in context—the assistant knows your parameters and live readouts.
landing.catalogLead: More subjects and labs can ship the same way—lesson metadata, metrics, and a scene.
landing.ctaTitle: Ready for your next class block?
landing.ctaText: Sign in (demo), pick an experiment, read the lesson, then run the sim.

auth.subtitle: Access the virtual laboratory to continue your experiments.

lab.intro: Explore how variables change outcomes. Open the lesson for objectives, then run the sim and ask the AI tutor.

lesson.title: Lesson
lesson.empty: No lesson content for this experiment yet.

ai.systemLanguageHint: Always respond in English. Keep answers short (2–4 sentences).
ai.title: AI Tutor
ai.welcome: I am your virtual lab assistant. Ask me anything about this experiment!
ai.error: Could not reach Gemini. Set GEMINI_API_KEY in .env (see .env.example), restart npm run dev, and use the dev server — the key is applied by the Vite proxy.
ai.fallback: Sorry, I could not get a response from the assistant.
ai.greeting: Hi! I'm LabSim Assistant — an AI tutor, not a human teacher. I'll help you explore this experiment by asking questions, not by giving you answers directly. Ready to start?

report.footnote: Note: The AI assistant in this session was an automated system, not a human teacher.

errors.missingApiKey: Add GEMINI_API_KEY to your .env file (Google AI Studio).

acidBase.name: Acid-Base Reaction
acidBase.subject: Chemistry
acidBase.aiContext: The student is using the Acid-Base Reaction experiment with pH {{ph}}, volume {{volume}} ml, liquid indicator regime {{indicator}}, temperature {{temperature}}°C, and bubble activity {{bubbles}}.

lesson.summary: Connect pH, indicator color, and simple solution behavior in a virtual flask—safe, repeatable, and tied to what you see in the beaker.
objectives: Explain how pH relates to acidity and basicity on the 1–14 scale.
Observe how volume changes the visual “fill” while pH drives color.
Use temperature and bubbles as secondary cues for vigorous vs calm conditions.

sections.title: Foundations
sections.body: pH is a logarithmic scale for hydrogen-ion activity in aqueous solutions. Near pH 7, solutions are roughly neutral; much lower is acidic, much higher is basic. Indicators change color across ranges to make that visible.

sections.title: In the simulation
sections.body: Drag pH and volume: the liquid tint tracks the indicator regime, and bubble activity increases for stronger acid or base in this simplified model—use it to spark discussion, not as a substitute for real titration data.

sections.title: Next steps in class
sections.body: Pair each observation with a prediction before you press Play. Ask: “If I raise pH by 2 units, what should happen to color and bubbling?”

pendulum.name: Simple Pendulum
pendulum.subject: Physics
pendulum.aiContext: The student is exploring an ideal pendulum. Current setup: Length {{length}}m, Mass {{mass}}kg, and Gravity {{gravity}} m/s². Calculated Period is {{period}}s and Frequency is {{frequency}}Hz.

lesson.summary: A simple pendulum’s period—for small swings—depends on length and gravitational field, not on the bob’s mass in the ideal model.
objectives: Use T ≈ 2π√(L/g) to predict how period changes when length or g changes.
Contrast the role of mass in the simulation (damping/size) vs ideal period.
Relate the motion you see to the computed period and frequency.

sections.title: Core ideas
sections.body: For small angles, the pendulum approximates simple harmonic motion with angular frequency ω = √(g/L). Heavier bobs don’t change T in the ideal point-mass model—though mass matters for real friction, string stretch, and energy.

sections.title: Try this
sections.body: Keep gravity at Earth-like values and double the length: period should increase. Then shorten the string and raise g (still within the slider)—period should drop.

sections.title: Where the model stops
sections.body: Large swing angles, air drag, and a flexible string break the small-angle approximation. Discuss which error matters first in a classroom demo.

projectile.name: Projectile Motion
projectile.subject: Physics
projectile.aiContext: The student is running a Projectile Motion experiment. Launch angle: {{angle}}°, initial speed: {{speed}} m/s, gravity: {{gravity}} m/s². The projectile travels {{range}} m horizontally, reaches a maximum height of {{maxHeight}} m, and lands after {{timeOfFlight}} seconds.

lesson.summary: Projectile motion separates into horizontal and vertical pieces: uniform motion sideways, uniform acceleration vertically. The range and peak height follow from those pieces.
objectives: Break initial velocity into components using the launch angle.
Predict how range changes when angle or speed changes (for a flat field).
Read off time of flight, apex height, and range coherently.

sections.title: Equations (flat field)
sections.body: With launch speed v and angle θ, vₓ = v cos θ and vᵧ = v sin θ. Time of flight for symmetric landing is 2vᵧ/g. Range R = vₓ × t. Max height occurs when vertical velocity hits zero.

sections.title: What to explore
sections.body: Fix speed and sweep angle: many students discover ~45° maximizes range on flat ground for a given v and g. Then fix angle and scale speed—range should grow roughly with v².

sections.title: Reality check
sections.body: We ignore air resistance. For light objects or high speeds, trajectories deviate—great discussion hook for modeling assumptions.

spring.name: Mass–Spring Oscillator
spring.subject: Physics
spring.aiContext: The student is using the Mass–Spring experiment with k={{k}} N/m, mass={{mass}} kg, amplitude={{amplitude}} m. Angular frequency ω={{omega}} rad/s, period={{period}} s, frequency={{frequency}} Hz.

lesson.summary: Explore simple harmonic motion: a mass attached to an ideal spring oscillates with a period that depends on stiffness and mass, not on amplitude (for ideal springs).
objectives: Relate angular frequency ω to k and m.
Predict how period changes when you increase mass or soften the spring.
Connect the 3D motion to the live period and frequency readouts.

sections.title: Core ideas
sections.body: For an ideal mass–spring system, ω = √(k/m). The period T = 2π/ω and frequency f = 1/T. A stiffer spring (larger k) raises ω and shortens T. A larger mass lowers ω and lengthens T.

sections.title: What to try
sections.body: Hold amplitude fixed: double the mass and observe the period. Then restore mass and double k—period should move the opposite way. Use Play to see the bob trace SHM while numbers update.

sections.title: Going further
sections.body: Real springs have damping and limits; our model assumes Hooke’s law and no friction. Discuss where that breaks down for real lab equipment.

circuitOhm.name: Ohm’s Law (DC)
circuitOhm.subject: Physics
circuitOhm.aiContext: The student is exploring Ohm’s law with V={{voltage}} V, R={{resistance}} Ω, so I={{current}} A and P={{power}} W.

lesson.summary: Ohm’s law relates voltage, current, and resistance in many DC circuits: V = I R. Power dissipated in the resistor is P = V I = I²R.
objectives: Compute current from V and R and interpret the value.
See how power changes when you hold one quantity fixed and vary the other.
Relate brighter “wire glow” in the sim to higher current.

sections.title: Core ideas
sections.body: For an ohmic resistor, I = V/R. If resistance doubles while voltage stays the same, current halves. Power P = V²/R = I²R—useful for intuition about heat and energy per second.

sections.title: What to try
sections.body: Set V = 12 V and sweep R from low to high: watch current and glow fall. Then fix R and raise V: current and power should rise (careful in real labs—components can overheat).

sections.title: Limits of the model
sections.body: Real batteries have internal resistance; wires aren’t perfect. The simulation highlights the ideal relationship so you can reason clearly before adding real-world corrections.
